statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries facing critical shortages in at least one fuel type, though overall reserves remain just above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
The EU's fuel security situation shows significant stress across multiple member states, with widespread critical shortages affecting individual fuel types despite overall reserves averaging above the 90-day mandatory minimum. Petrol reserves average 98.9 days, diesel 97.8 days, and jet fuel 91.9 days, providing only narrow buffers above emergency thresholds.

The geographic distribution of shortages reveals particular vulnerability in Central and Eastern Europe. Romania faces the most severe situation with all three fuel types in critical status - petrol at just 33.2 days, diesel at 32.4 days, and jet fuel at 76.4 days. Poland, Luxembourg, and Germany also show critical levels across all fuel categories, with Germany's diesel reserves at only 64.2 days despite being Europe's largest economy.

Jet fuel presents the most widespread concern, with 19 of 27 countries showing critical or warning status, reflecting the sector's particular vulnerability to supply disruptions. The aviation fuel shortage is most acute in Poland (21.3 days) and Portugal (23.1 days). Meanwhile, some countries maintain strong reserves - Slovenia and Estonia both hold 365 days of jet fuel, and Finland maintains 247 days of diesel reserves.

Current Brent crude prices at $105.33 per barrel, up 27% since the Middle East conflict began, are contributing to elevated fuel costs across the bloc. Average petrol prices have reached €1.764 per liter, with significant variation from Malta's €1.34 to the Netherlands' €2.279, indicating varying national tax policies and supply chain pressures.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries face critical shortages in at least one fuel type, with Romania, Poland, Luxembourg and Germany showing critical levels across all three fuels
Jet fuel reserves are most concerning bloc-wide, with 19 countries at critical or warning levels and an EU average of just 91.9 days
Central and Eastern European countries show the greatest vulnerability, with Romania's reserves as low as 32.4 days for diesel
Brent crude at $105.33 per barrel represents a 27% increase since the Middle East conflict began, contributing to price pressures
Despite individual country challenges, EU-wide averages remain above the 90-day mandatory minimum, providing limited systemic buffer
